title: Grouting ceramic tiles
subtitle: Press in, clean back, polish when hazed
excerpt: How to grout ceramic wall and floor tiles: mixing the grout, spreading and pressing it fully into the joints with a float, cleaning the tile face in stages, and sealing the finished joints for a durable result.
sourceNotes: British Standard BS 5385 Part 1. Mapei and BAL grouting application guides. Silicone sealant joint guidance from the Tile Association UK.

content.text: Grouting is the last step in any tile job and the one most often rushed. Grout pressed in carelessly leaves voids that fill with water and harbour mould. Grout cleaned off too early moves in the joint; cleaned too late, it bonds to the tile face and requires an acid wash to remove. The process has a short but definite working window; read the set as you go.
What you'll need
Grout.
Match the grout type to the joint width: unsanded grout for joints up to 3 mm; sanded grout for joints 3 to 12 mm. Epoxy grout for kitchen worktops and wet rooms where chemical resistance is needed.
Grout float.
A rubber-faced float for spreading and pressing grout into joints.
Sponge and bucket of clean water.
For cleaning the tile face in stages. Change the water frequently.
Dry cloth.
For polishing the grout haze once set.
Silicone sealant (colour-matched).
For the movement joints at all internal corners and where the tiling meets a bath, worktop, or shower tray. These joints must be flexible; a grouted corner joint cracks.
Preparation
Step 1, check the adhesive is fully cured.
Allow 24 hours minimum after laying before grouting. Moving tiles in fresh adhesive breaks the bond. Remove all tile spacers.
Step 2, clean the joints.
Remove any adhesive squeeze-up from inside the joints with the utility knife or a pointed stick. Joints full of adhesive cannot hold grout to their full depth.
Grouting
Step 3, mix the grout.
Add grout powder to water (not water to powder) and mix to a firm, lump-free paste. Mix only what you can apply in 20 minutes.
Step 4, spread and press the grout.

Load the grout float and spread grout diagonally across the tiles, pressing firmly so the grout is packed fully into the joints. Work in 1-square-metre sections. Drag the float edge at 45 degrees to the joints to remove the excess grout from the tile faces, leaving grout only in the joints.
Step 5, clean the tile faces.

Wait 10 to 15 minutes for the grout to begin setting before cleaning. Wipe the tile surface with a barely damp sponge in circular strokes, rinsing the sponge frequently. Do not flood the joints with water at this stage; this weakens the grout. Work lightly; the aim is to remove the grout from the tile face while leaving the joints full.
Step 6, shape the joints.

While the grout is still pliable, run a rounded stick, a grout shaper, or the back of a bent finger along each joint to give a slightly concave profile. This presses the grout surface down and makes it denser and more water-resistant.
Step 7, polish the haze.
Once the grout has hardened (1 to 2 hours for most products), polish the remaining grout haze from the tile faces with a dry cloth.
Movement joints
Step 8, seal the movement joints with silicone.

Do not grout internal corners, the joint where tiles meet a bath or shower tray, or any joint where two different materials meet. These are movement joints. Fill them with a colour-matched silicone sealant after the grout has cured fully. Mask each side with tape before applying, smooth with a wet finger, and remove the tape before the silicone skins.
Common mistakes
Grout haze that will not clean off.

Left too long before cleaning, or too much water was used in the sponge stage and the grout was rewetted and smeared. A proprietary grout haze remover deals with most cases; an acid-based cleaner for stubborn cases, with full ventilation and gloves.
Grout crumbling from the joints.
Usually too much water in the mix, or the grout was not pressed in firmly. Rake out the joint with a grout rake tool, vacuum out the dust, and re-grout.
